title: Latch-hook flower cushion
subtitle: A simple flower design in pre-cut rug wool on latch-hook canvas, made up as a cushion cover
excerpt: Work a stylised flower motif on standard 5-holes-per-inch latch-hook canvas using pre-cut rug wool lengths. The finished panel is made up as a cushion cover with a fabric back, fitted to a 40 x 40 cm cushion pad.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional latch-hook craft practice. Latch-hook flower cushion designs are a classic British craft project documented in latch-hook kits and guild instruction books from the 1970s through to current practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hook tool with a small hinged latch that opens to catch a folded length of rug wool and close around it as the hook is drawn back through the canvas, knotting the wool onto the canvas threads.
A stiff, open-weave mesh fabric designed for latch-hook work. Standard canvas has 5 holes per inch (about 2 holes per cm); each hole takes one folded length of pre-cut rug wool.

content.text: Latch-hook work is one of the most accessible fibre arts: the
is a clear grid, and each action takes about 3 seconds. A flower motif uses 3 to 5 colours and produces a dense, textural surface well suited to a cushion front.

items.name: Pre-cut rug wool (pink or coral for flower petals)
Pre-cut rug wool (yellow for flower centre)
Pre-cut rug wool (green for stems and leaves)
Fabric for backing (heavy cotton or linen, 45 x 45 cm)
Carpet tape or binding tape for finishing

content.text: Step 1: Mark the design on the canvas

Draw a simple flower in waterproof marker. Draw a circle about 5 cm across for the centre. Add 6 to 8 petals reaching about 12 cm total. Add two leaves on a stem. The canvas grid makes it easy to count holes and keep the design even.
Step 2: Work the design areas first

Fold a length of pre-cut rug wool in half. Hold the fold over the canvas thread with your non-hook hand. Insert the latch hook from front to back through the canvas hole below the thread, bringing the hook up through the fold of wool. Place both wool tails over the hook and close the latch. Pull the hook back through the canvas hole; the latch opens as it passes through, then closes to catch the tails and knot them through the fold. Tug gently to firm the knot. Work the flower petals first, then the centre, then the leaves and stem.
Fill all remaining canvas holes with the cream background colour, working in rows from bottom to top. Every hole in the 40 x 40 cm panel area should be filled.
Step 4: Finish and make up the cushion cover

Fold the unworked canvas margins to the back of the panel and secure with carpet tape. Place the panel right-side-up on the backing fabric. Stitch together around three sides with a strong thread, then turn right-side out through the open side. Insert the cushion pad and hand-stitch the final side closed, or leave it open as an envelope closure.

items.problem: The wool tails pull out of the canvas after knotting
items.cause: The latch hook was not fully closed when drawing back through the canvas
items.fix: Ensure the latch closes over both tails of the wool before pulling the hook back. The tails must be completely inside the latch for the knot to form.

items.problem: The finished panel is stiff and hard rather than soft
items.cause: The canvas is a stiff mesh; the woolwork softens the surface but the canvas remains rigid
items.fix: This is normal for latch-hook panels. Backing the panel with soft fabric and leaving the canvas intact is the standard construction for latch-hook cushion covers.
